text: Here we show that hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) can be produced via solar-driven catalysis using water (H2O) and dioxygen (O2) as inexpensive and abundant resources, paving the way for sustainable solar fuel production. We have established a visible light-promoted photocatalytic process to produce H2O2 under acidic aqueous conditions by combining a Ru photosensitizer (complex [RuII(Me2phen)3]2+ with various water oxidation catalysts (WOCs). Upon photoexcitation the Ru complex oxidatively quenches O2 into the highly reactive superoxide radical anion (O2•–) and H2O2 is formed as the result of the subsequent disproportionation of O2•–, while the reduced complex [RuIII(Me2phen)3]3+ is re-reduced back by H2O using the added WOC—Ir(OH)3 nanoparticles. The addition of Sc3+ to the reaction media allows to suppress the back electron transfer between the reduced Ru complex and O2•–, as elucidated by transient absorption and EPR spectroscopy. By replacing the Ir(OH)3 nanoparticles as a WOC with a homogeneous Co complex—[CoIII(Cp*))(bpy)(H2O)]2+ we achieve a 37% quantum yield at 450 nm and a corresponding solar-to-chemical energy conversion efficiency of 0.25% at neutral pH confirming the feasibility of producing H2O2 in a carbon-neutral manner using solar energy and abundant feedstocks.
